name: spleen
description: encased in a thick connective-tissue capsule
location: left side of the abdominal cavity

tissues.name: red pulp
tissues.descripton: a network of splenic cords (cords of Billroth) and sinusoids (wide vessels) filled with blood
function: splenic cords serve as important reservoirs for large quantities of macrophages and other phagocytic white blood cells called monocytes

major site of the destruction of red blood cells, which normally have a life span of only 120 days: the hemoglobin that they contain is degraded to a readily excretable pigment and an iron molecule that is recycled (i.e., used to produce new hemoglobin elsewhere)

tissues.name: white pulp
tissues.description: lymphoid tissue that usually surrounds splenic blood vessels
tissues.contains: typical lymphoid elements, such as plasma cells, lymphocytes, and lymphatic nodules, called follicles

functions: the primary filtering element for the blood
storing and releasing certain types of immune cells that mediate tissue inflammation

react to microorganisms and other antigens that reach the bloodstream by releasing special phagocytic cells known as macrophages, which reside in both red and white pulp, and serve to remove foreign material from the blood and to initiate an immune reaction that results in the production of antibodies

name: kidney
description: a paired reddish-brown multilobar multipapillary bean-shaped blood-filtering organ whose functional units are called nephrons, above a million in each kidney

parts.name: cortex
parts.location: arches over the bases of the pyramids and fills gaps between the pyramids

comprises.name: renal pyramid
comprises.orientation: with bases outward and apexes projecting, either singly or in groups, into the renal sinus

parts.name: hilus
parts.leads-to: a cavity within the kidney known as the renal (kidney) sinus
parts.function: the point of entry and exit of the renal arteries and veins, lymphatic vessels, nerves, and the enlarged upper extension of the ureters

parts.name: renal papilla
parts.description: each projection of one or more pyramid apexes into the sinus
parts.appearance: the bases are irregular, with slender striations extending toward the external kidney surface

functional-unit.name: nephron
functional-unit.working: Blood flows into and away from the glomerulus through tiny arteries called arterioles, which reach and leave the glomerulus through the open end of the capsule. In the renal corpuscle, fluid filters out of the blood in the glomerulus through the inner wall of the capsule and into the nephron tubule. As this filtrate passes through the tubule, its composition is altered by the secretion of certain substances into it and by the selective reabsorption of water and other constituents from it. The final product is urine, which is conveyed through the collecting tubules into the renal pelvis.

connected-to.name: ureter
connected-to.functions: carry excreted urine to the bladder

functions.name: control of the volume of various body fluids
reabsorb water and nutrients (sodium, bicarbonate, glucose, and amino acids)
convert a precursor of vitamin D to its active form, calcitriol
synthesize the hormones erythropoietin and renin
secrete wastes (e.g. hydrogen, ammonium, potassium and uric acid), producing the final urine, which is expelled

name: liver
description: a dark reddish brown, wedge-shaped organ with two lobes of unequal size and shape; a major metabolic organ exclusively found in vertebrates, which performs many essential biological functions
location: in the right upper quadrant of the abdomen
mostly shielded by the lower right rib cage
functions: synthesis of various proteins and various other biochemicals necessary for digestion (e.g. bile) and growth
the production of a number of hormones
conversion and storage of nutrients such as glucose and glycogen
notes: estimates regarding the organ's total number of functions vary, but is generally cited as being around 500; for this reason, the liver has sometimes been described as the body's "chemical factory"

name: pancreas
location: with the head lying immediately adjacent to the duodenum

connected-to.name: duct of Wirsung
connected-to.functions: collect pancreatic juice and empty into the duodenum

connected-to.name: duct of Santorini
connected-to.functions: collect pancreatic juice and empty into the duodenum

functions.name: endocrine
functions.description: the islets of Langerhans secrete hormones, including insulin and glucagon, directly into the bloodstream to regulate carbohydrate, fat, and protein metabolism
regulated-by: circulating metabolites (e.g., glucose, amino acids, ketone bodies)

functions.name: digestive exocrine
functions.description: acinar cells in the pancreas secrete digestive enzymes into the small intestine via the pancreatic ducts to break down dietary proteins, fats, and carbohydrates

notes: as an endocrine gland, it functions mostly to regulate blood sugar levels, secreting the hormones insulin, glucagon, somatostatin and pancreatic polypeptide

As a part of the digestive system, it functions as an exocrine gland secreting pancreatic juice into the duodenum through the pancreatic duct. This juice contains bicarbonate, which neutralizes acid entering the duodenum from the stomach; and digestive enzymes, which break down carbohydrates, proteins and fats in food entering the duodenum from the stomach

[their flow] is controlled by the vagus nerve and by the hormones secretin and cholecystokinin, which are produced in the intestinal mucosa. When food enters the duodenum, secretin and cholecystokinin are released into the bloodstream by secretory cells of the duodenum. When these hormones reach the pancreas, the pancreatic cells are stimulated to produce and release large amounts of water, bicarbonate, and digestive enzymes, which then flow into the intestine

name: gallbladder
location: just under the right lobe of liver
functions: stores and concentrates the bile produced by the liver
notes: bile is later excreted to the duodenum
